mnemonics.title: Five Characteristics of Cloud
mnemonics.content: The acronym 'OBRERM' helps remember: On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Elasticity, Measured service. Think: 'On-demand Broad Resources Elicit Metered bills'.

mnemonics.title: Service Models Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: IaaS = Infrastructure (bottom - you manage OS/apps), PaaS = Platform (middle - you manage apps), SaaS = Software (top - provider manages everything). Remember: 'I' for 'I'm in control of infrastructure', 'P' for 'Programming platform', 'S' for 'Software is served to you'.

mnemonics.title: CapEx vs OpEx
mnemonics.content: CapEx = Capital Expenditure = Big upfront buy (like buying a car). OpEx = Operational Expenditure = Pay as you go (like paying for taxi/Uber per ride). Cloud shifts from CapEx to OpEx.

acronyms.usage: Upfront investment in physical assets like servers, datacenters, and software licenses. Traditional IT model requiring large initial capital.
Pay-as-you-go spending on consumed resources. Cloud computing converts CapEx to OpEx, reducing upfront costs.
Virtualized computing resources including servers, storage, and networking. Users manage OS, middleware, runtime, and data.
Development and deployment platform provided by cloud. Users develop applications; provider manages infrastructure, OS, and runtime.
Software applications delivered over the internet. Provider manages everything; users access via web browser.

acronyms.expansion: National Institute of Standards and Technology
acronyms.usage: US agency that defines cloud computing standards and essential characteristics used worldwide.

visualTips.visualization: Draw an inverted pyramid: Top (smallest) = SaaS with user only seeing software; Middle = PaaS with user seeing platform for coding; Bottom (largest) = IaaS with user seeing full infrastructure stack. Label each layer with what provider vs user manages.

Draw two graphs: Traditional shows high initial spike (CapEx) then maintenance costs; Cloud shows low starting point with gradual upward slope based on usage (OpEx). The area under curves represents total cost.

Picture an apartment building (physical infrastructure) with multiple tenants (customers). Each tenant has their own locked apartment (isolated data), but shares building facilities (pooled resources). Virtual walls keep tenants separate.

analogies.analogy: Like hiring a taxi - you pay only for the distance traveled and time used, without owning the vehicle. Similarly, cloud users pay only for computing resources consumed.
Think of a bank safe deposit box. You don't own the vault (physical storage), but you get secure, dedicated space (your data) that you can access when needed, paying for the service.
An elastic band stretches and contracts as needed. Cloud resources do the same - expanding during high demand (stretching) and shrinking during low demand (contracting), automatically.
A shared office workspace where multiple companies use the same building, meeting rooms, and facilities, but each has their own private office spaces and equipment.
